disease.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis and Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State

emergencyPriority.notes: Life-threatening metabolic emergency requiring immediate fluid resuscitation, insulin therapy, and correction of electrolyte abnormalities. DKA mortality 1-5%; HHS mortality 10-20%.

mustNotMiss: Cerebral edema in pediatric DKA: headache, bradycardia, hypertension, altered mental status, Cushing triad
Severe hypokalemia before insulin initiation (life-threatening arrhythmia on first insulin dose)
Osmotic demyelination syndrome from overly rapid correction of hyponatremia/hyperosmolality
Hidden sepsis or myocardial infarction as precipitating cause in DKA without obvious trigger
Insulin pump malfunction or failure in type 1 diabetics with recurrent DKA
Nausea/vomiting with inability to tolerate PO: rapid progression to severe DKA
diagnosticClues: DKA triad: hyperglycemia > 250 mg/dL, metabolic acidosis (pH < 7.3, HCO3 < 18 mEq/L), ketonemia (beta-hydroxybutyrate > 3 mmol/L)
HHS: glucose > 600 mg/dL, effective osmolality > 320 mOsm/kg, pH > 7.3, HCO3 > 18, no significant ketosis
Anion gap metabolic acidosis with increased osmolar gap suggests alcoholic or starvation ketoacidosis
Pseudohyponatremia: Na decreases 1.6 mEq/L per 100 mg/dL glucose rise above 100
Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing) indicate severe metabolic acidosis
exclusionClues: pH > 7.3 and HCO3 > 20 with normal anion gap effectively excludes DKA
Negative urine/serum ketones and normal anion gap excludes DKA
Normal glucose < 200 mg/dL makes hyperglycemic emergency unlikely (consider other causes of acidosis)
Euglycemic DKA can occur with SGLT2 inhibitors: check ketones even with normal glucose

tools.name: DKA Severity Classification
classification: Mild DKA: pH 7.25-7.30, HCO3 15-18, mild ketonemia, alert
Moderate DKA: pH 7.0-7.24, HCO3 10-14, moderate ketonemia, drowsy but arousable
Severe DKA: pH < 7.0, HCO3 < 10, severe ketonemia, obtunded/coma

tools.name: HHS Severity Classification
classification: Mild HHS: osmolality 320-330, alert, 6-9 L deficit
Moderate HHS: osmolality 330-350, drowsy, 9-12 L deficit
Severe HHS: osmolality > 350, obtunded/coma, > 12 L deficit

historyFeatures: Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, weakness (classic osmotic symptoms)
Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain (common in DKA, can mimic acute abdomen)
Precipitating event: infection (30-40%), insulin omission (20-30%), new-onset diabetes (15-25%), MI, stroke, pancreatitis, surgery, trauma, drugs (cocaine, SGLT2i, antipsychotics)
Type 1 vs Type 2 diabetes duration, prior DKA episodes, usual insulin regimen
Comorbidities: CAD, CKD, CHF (influence fluid resuscitation)
Menstrual history: DKA more frequent premenstrually
examFeatures: General: dehydrated appearance, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, poor skin turgor
Vitals: tachycardia, hypotension (dehydration), Kussmaul respirations (metabolic acidosis), hypothermia
Abdomen: diffuse tenderness without rigidity (gastroparesis from acidosis)
Neurologic: drowsiness, obtundation, focal deficits (cerebral edema in children), areflexia (hypokalemia/hypermagnesemia)
Skin: acanthosis nigricans (type 2), lipodystrophy (insulin injection sites)
riskFactors: Type 1 diabetes (absolute insulin deficiency, more prone to DKA)
Poor glycemic control (A1c > 9%) or frequent prior DKA admissions
Non-adherence to insulin regimen (cost barriers, depression, eating disorders)
SGLT2 inhibitor use (euglycemic DKA, especially perioperatively)

subtypes.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
subtypes.pathophysiology: Absolute/relative insulin deficiency with increased counterregulatory hormones leading to unrestrained lipolysis, ketogenesis, and metabolic acidosis

subtypes.name: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)
subtypes.pathophysiology: Relative insulin deficiency sufficient to prevent ketosis but not hyperglycemia; profound osmotic diuresis with extreme hyperosmolality

subtypes.name: Mixed DKA/HHS
subtypes.pathophysiology: Combined features of both DKA and HHS with acidosis and severe hyperosmolality

mimics: Starvation ketoacidosis (mild ketosis, normal glucose, normal or low anion gap)
Alcoholic ketoacidosis (history of binge drinking, vomiting, normal or low glucose)
Lactic acidosis (elevated lactate > 5 mmol/L, normal or mildly elevated glucose, no ketones)
Chronic kidney disease with uremic acidosis (low HCO3, elevated Cr, normal glucose, no ketones)
Salicylate poisoning (mixed respiratory alkalosis + metabolic acidosis, tinnitus, elevated salicylate level)
Toxic alcohol poisoning (methanol, ethylene glycol, isopropanol) with elevated osmolar gap

investigations.detail: Primary ketone measure. > 3 mmol/L confirms DKA. Beta-hydroxybutyrate is the predominant ketone body in DKA. Urine acetoacetate may be negative early (only detects acetoacetate, not beta-hydroxybutyrate).
Glucose, Na (corrected for hyperglycemia), K, Cl, HCO3, BUN, Cr, Ca, Mg, Phos. Calculate anion gap (Nl 8-12). Calculate effective osmolality: 2(Na+Cm) + glucose/18.
pH, pCO2, HCO3, base deficit. Venous pH correlates well with arterial for DKA monitoring. Serial pH q2-4h to track resolution.
Assess for hyperkalemia (peaked T waves, widened QRS) or hypokalemia (U waves, flat T, PVCs). May show evidence of silent MI as DKA trigger.
CBC with differential, blood cultures x2, urinalysis + culture, chest X-ray, CRP/procalcitonin. Search for precipitating infection.
Elevated in 15-20% of DKA (often hyperlipasemia without clinical pancreatitis). Must differentiate from acute pancreatitis as DKA trigger.

pathophysiology.summary: Absolute or relative insulin deficiency combined with elevated counterregulatory hormones (glucagon, catecholamines, cortisol, growth hormone) results in uncontrolled hepatic glucose production, decreased peripheral glucose uptake, and activation of lipolysis with ketone body production leading to metabolic acidosis.
mechanisms: Insulin deficiency decreases GLUT4 translocation, reduces peripheral glucose utilization
Glucagon excess activates carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 (CPT-1), shunting free fatty acids toward ketogenesis in liver mitochdria
Beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate accumulation causes anion gap metabolic acidosis
Osmotic diuresis from glucosuria leads to profound water and electrolyte losses

complications: Cerebral edema: leading cause of DKA mortality in children, peak at 4-12 hours of treatment
Hypokalemia: most dangerous electrolyte complication; potassium shifts intracellularly with insulin, dextrose, and bicarbonate
Hypophosphatemia: can cause rhabdomyolysis, diaphragmatic weakness, impaired cardiac contractility
Hypoglycemia from overly aggressive insulin therapy
Non-anion gap metabolic acidosis from aggressive saline resuscitation (hyperchloremic acidosis)
ARDS from aggressive fluid resuscitation or underlying pneumonia

management.detail: Phase 1: 1-2 L 0.9% NS over 1-2 hours. Phase 2: 0.45% NS at 250-500 mL/hr based on corrected Na and volume status. Phase 3: 5% dextrose in 0.45% NS when glucose < 200-250 (DKA) or < 300 (HHS). Total deficit: 6-9 L. Goal: replace 50% of deficit in 8 hours, remainder over 24 hours. Per Joint British Diabetes Societies (JBDS) and ADA guidelines.

IV regular insulin: 0.1 U/kg bolus (optional in HHS) then 0.1 U/kg/hr continuous infusion. When glucose < 200 (DKA) or < 300 (HHS), reduce to 0.02-0.05 U/kg/hr AND add D5-10% IV fluids. Titrate to decrease glucose 50-75 mg/dL/hr. Continue until acidosis resolves (pH > 7.3, HCO3 > 18, AG normal). Never stop insulin without dextrose-containing fluids.

Check K before insulin initiation. If K < 3.3 mEq/L: HOLD insulin, give 20-40 mEq K/hr until K > 3.3. If K 3.3-5.0: give 20-30 mEq per liter IV fluids. If K > 5.0: do not replace, monitor q2h. Goal K 4.0-5.0. Check K every 2 hours during active titration.

Routine bicarbonate NOT recommended (Cochrane review 2011, GARDIAL study). May worsen hypokalemia, cause paradoxical CSF acidosis, shift O2 dissociation curve. Consider only if pH < 6.9 with life-threatening hyperkalemia or severe acidosis impairing cardiac contractility. Give 50-100 mEq NaHCO3 in 400 mL sterile water over 2 hours.

Risk factors: severe acidosis, high BUN, severe hypocapnia. Prevention: avoid rapid correction of osmolality (maintain gradual glucose decline 50-75 mg/dL/hr), use isotonic fluids, avoid bicarbonate. Mannitol or 3% hypertonic saline if signs of cerebral edema develop.

Only when DKA/HHS resolved: pH > 7.3, HCO3 > 18, AG normal, eating. Give long-acting insulin 2-4 hours before stopping IV insulin infusion. Overlap is critical to prevent recurrence of ketosis. Restart home regimen at 80-100% of usual dose.

Even larger fluid deficits (10-15 L). Use 0.9% NS more aggressively initially then 0.45% NS. Lower insulin doses (0.05 U/kg/hr without bolus). More gradual glucose correction. Higher threshold for adding dextrose (glucose < 300). Monitor osmolality closely: target decline < 3-4 mOsm/kg/hr.
